request.question: Which rock group has more band members in its core group, White Magic or Pale Saints?

title: Pale Saints
La Mar Enfortuna (band)
Mormon folklore
Opus Pro
White Rose
White Witch (band)
Edsel Auctioneer
List of the Doobie Brothers band members
White Magic (band)
Slow Buildings
sentences: Pale Saints were a British alternative rock band formed in 1987 by Ian Masters (bass/vocals), Graeme Naysmith (guitar), and Chris Cooper (drums) in Leeds, England.
The group began as a jangly indie pop band, influenced by Primal Scream's early sound.
La Mar Enfortuna is the Sephardic side project from the alt rock group Elysian Fields, Oren Bloedow and Jennifer Charles.

La Mar Enfortuna is a modern interpretation of lost or forgotten music, mostly of the Sephardim, from the 11th to the 16th century, with songs sung in Ladino, Arabic, Aramaic, Spanish, Greek, and English.
They incorporate the sounds of jazz, folk, rock, Middle Eastern, and Latin musics.
Besides Charles and Bloedow, the core group as it now stands includes Doug Wieselman, Ted Reichman, Robert DiPietro, and Brahim Fribgane.
The group released two albums, an eponymous album in 2001 and "Convivencia" in 2007, both on John Zorn's Tzadik label.
Guests on "Convivencia" include Ljova Zhurbin, Chuscales, Liaqat Khan, and Benjamin Lapidus.
The band has played in Washington, DC, New York City, at La Cigale in Paris, La Huerta de Federico Garcia Lorca in Granada, Spain, and the Chicago World Music Festival.
Mormon folklore is a body of expressive culture unique to The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church) and its members.
Mormon folklore includes tales, oral history, popular beliefs, customs, music, jokes, and material culture traditions.
In folklore studies, Mormons can be seen as a regional group, since the core group of Mormon settlers in Utah had a common religion and had to modify their surroundings for survival.
Opus Pro is a Latvian heavy rock group, established in 1986.
The core group consists of lead singer Oleg Andreyev (Alex), and guitarist and songwriter Harry Zarins.
Opus Pro was one of the most popular Latvian groups in the 1980s and 1990s.
The band released two original albums "Dzimene" and "I'm going to steal you" and a "Greatest Hits".
The White Rose (German: "die Weiße Rose" ) was a non-violent, intellectual resistance group in Nazi Germany led by a group of students and a professor at the University of Munich.
The group conducted an anonymous leaflet and graffiti campaign which called for active opposition to the Nazi regime.
Their activities started in Munich on June 27th, 1942, and ended with the arrest of the core group by the Gestapo on February 18th, 1943.

They, as well as other members and supporters of the group who carried on distributing the pamphlets, faced show trials by the Nazi People's Court ("Volksgerichtshof"), and many of them were sentenced to death or imprisonment.
White Witch was a glam rock/psychedelic/hard rock band from Tampa, Florida that made two albums for Capricorn Records in the early 1970s.
Their name was a paean to "white magic", contrary to the "black magic" of groups like Black Sabbath.

As the band announced before their shows: "To bring good where there once was evil, to bring love where there once was hate, to bring wisdom where there once was ignorance; this is the power of White Witch".
The Edsel Auctioneer were a band formed in Leeds in 1988 by Ashley Horner (guitar/ vocals), Phil Pettler (bass/ vocals), Aidan Winterburn (vocals, guitar) and Chris Cooper (drums).
They were named after the ill-fated Ford automobile whose front grille was supposed to have resembled a woman's pudenda.
Best friends with Pale Saints, they lived on the same street in Leeds, Harold Avenue (which spawned the so-called Voice of the Harolds).
The American band The Doobie Brothers has had a plethora of members since its inception in 1970, including guitarist/singer Tom Johnston and keyboardist/singer Michael McDonald.
The Doobie Brothers employ official members and a small group of touring musicians, usually with a core group shown in promotional material.
Guitarist/singer Patrick Simmons is the only constant member of the group, and the only one who has appeared on all of their releases.
White Magic is a psychedelic folk rock group formed in Brooklyn, New York City, and led by singer/guitarist/pianist/composer Mira Billotte.
Billotte performs under this moniker both with accompaniment or solo, using a daf, shruti box, and singing a cappella.
She is a modern trobairitz and, invoking both traditional and experimental folk, White Magic's sound ranges from loud psychedelia to meditative trance.
Slow Buildings is the third and final studio album from Pale Saints, released by 4AD on 29 August 1994.

This was the band's first album following the departure of original singer and bassist Ian Masters and addition of Colleen Browne on bass/vocals; guitarist Meriel Barham, who had joined Pale Saints for their previous album "In Ribbons", also took over lead vocal duties.
"Slow Buildings" features the singles "Fine Friend", "Henry", "One Blue Hill", and "Angel (Will You Be My)", with the latter three songs released as radio singles only.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

hop1_docs: Here We Go Magic | Here We Go Magic is an American indie rock band based in Brooklyn, New York.  The group was originally formed by core members Luke Temple, Michael Bloch and Peter Hale, in late 2008.  The project signed to Western Vinyl in 2008, followed by the five-piece group's signing to Secretly Canadian in 2009.

Pale Saints | Pale Saints were a British alternative rock band formed in 1987 by Ian Masters (bass/vocals), Graeme Naysmith (guitar), and Chris Cooper (drums) in Leeds, England.  The group began as a jangly indie pop band, influenced by Primal Scream's early sound.

White Witch (band) | White Witch was a glam rock/psychedelic/hard rock band from Tampa, Florida that made two albums for Capricorn Records in the early 1970s.  Their name was a paean to "white magic", contrary to the "black magic" of groups like Black Sabbath.  As the band announced before their shows: "To bring good where there once was evil, to bring love where there once was hate, to bring wisdom where there once was ignorance; this is the power of White Witch".

Principal Edwards Magic Theatre | Principal Edwards Magic Theatre was an English performance art collective in the United Kingdom made up of about 14 musicians, poets, dancers, and sound and lighting technicians.  It existed between 1968 and 1971, after which core members formed a more conventional rock band under the shortened name Principal Edwards.

Damn (band) | Damn!  is a funk-rock band based in Lund, Sweden.  The four core members are joined on stage and on record by a collective of DJs and live musicians.  The group's first album "Natural Sounds" was recorded by the group as a trio and released on Lund Records in Sweden and LoveCat Music in North America.  The band members are also videographers and direct their own videos.  The band has toured Japan, Europe and North America.

List of the Doobie Brothers band members | The American band The Doobie Brothers has had a plethora of members since its inception in 1970, including guitarist/singer Tom Johnston and keyboardist/singer Michael McDonald.  The Doobie Brothers employ official members and a small group of touring musicians, usually with a core group shown in promotional material.  Guitarist/singer Patrick Simmons is the only constant member of the group, and the only one who has appeared on all of their releases.

White Magic (band) | White Magic is a psychedelic folk rock group formed in Brooklyn, New York City, and led by singer/guitarist/pianist/composer Mira Billotte.  Billotte performs under this moniker both with accompaniment or solo, using a daf, shruti box, and singing a cappella.  She is a modern trobairitz and, invoking both traditional and experimental folk, White Magic's sound ranges from loud psychedelia to meditative trance.

response.summary_1:
Primary topics: White Magic (band) and Pale Saints (band)

- Pale Saints — formed in 1987; founding members: Ian Masters (bass/vocals), Graeme Naysmith (guitar), Chris Cooper (drums).  
- White Magic — "led by" Mira Billotte; described as a project where Billotte "performs under this moniker both with accompaniment or solo." (no explicit core-member count given).  
- Missing / ambiguous fact: "Number of core members" (core group size) for White Magic is not stated in the passages; bridge entity = White Magic (Mira Billotte). Pale Saints' founding trio implies three core members in the provided text, but full "core group" roster/infobox not confirmed.  
- Next-hop retrieval (priority order):
  1) White Magic (band) — fetch lede/infobox and "Members"/"Personnel"/"History" sections to obtain core group size or official lineup (search term: "White Magic (band) members" or "White Magic Mira Billotte lineup").  
  2) Pale Saints — fetch lede/infobox and "Members"/"Original lineup" or "History" sections to confirm official/core membership beyond the founding trio.  
  3) If needed, search "Mira Billotte collaborators" or "White Magic lineup" for interviews/credits listing regular contributors.
hop2_docs: White Witch (band) | White Witch was a glam rock/psychedelic/hard rock band from Tampa, Florida that made two albums for Capricorn Records in the early 1970s.  Their name was a paean to "white magic", contrary to the "black magic" of groups like Black Sabbath.  As the band announced before their shows: "To bring good where there once was evil, to bring love where there once was hate, to bring wisdom where there once was ignorance; this is the power of White Witch".

White Magic (band) | White Magic is a psychedelic folk rock group formed in Brooklyn, New York City, and led by singer/guitarist/pianist/composer Mira Billotte.  Billotte performs under this moniker both with accompaniment or solo, using a daf, shruti box, and singing a cappella.  She is a modern trobairitz and, invoking both traditional and experimental folk, White Magic's sound ranges from loud psychedelia to meditative trance.

White magic | White magic has traditionally referred to the use of supernatural or powers or magic for selfless purposes.  With respect to the philosophy of left-hand path and right-hand path, white magic is the benevolent counterpart of malicious black magic.  Because of its ties to traditional pagan nature worship, white magic is often also referred to as "natural magic".

Jeff Cotton | Jeffrey Ralph Cotton (born May 31, 1949) is an American rock guitarist, known for his work as a member of Captain Beefheart's Magic Band.  Cotton first came to attention as guitarist with Merrell and the Exiles, who had a few local hits in 1964 in the Los Angeles area.  He subsequently joined Blues in a Bottle, which also featured future Magic Band members Mark Boston, Bill Harkleroad and John French.  He was recruited into the Magic Band in 1967 as a replacement for Ry Cooder.

Magic Hour (band) | Magic Hour were an American psychedelic rock band from greater Boston, Massachusetts, USA and were made up of former Crystalized Movements member Wayne Rogers and Kate Biggar and former Galaxie 500 members Damon Krukowski and Naomi Yang.

White Magic for Lovers | White Magic for Lovers is the second album released by the London-based band Drugstore.  It reached the UK albums chart at number 45.  The album was noted for the "Song for Pessoa", about the Portuguese modernist poet Fernando Pessoa, "The Funeral" and the song "El President", a duet between Isabel Monteiro and Radiohead's Thom Yorke, which reached the UK singles chart at number 20.

Magic (Pilot song) | "Magic" is a popular 1974 song by Scottish pop rock band Pilot, and was the first hit single for the group.  It was written by band members Billy Lyall and David Paton for their debut album, "From the Album of the Same Name".
response.summary_2: Pale Saints has more core members than White Magic: Pale Saints' founding lineup is a trio (Ian Masters, Graeme Naysmith, Chris Cooper). White Magic is led by Mira Billotte and is essentially Billotte's project, often performed solo or with accompaniment.
